      Bond trading
   =20
 =20

Traditionally there are three main things you need=C2=A0to be a good bond d=
ealer:
=09
A listed phone number. As a dealer, your job is to buy bonds from people wh=
o want to sell them, and sell bonds to people who want to buy them. You rel=
y on customers for your flow, and bonds have historically been a voice-trad=
ed market. You need customers to know that you are in the bond-dealing busi=
ness, and to think of you when they want to trade bonds.=C2=A0=09
Some idea of what bonds are worth. If the customers do call you up looking =
to sell you some bonds, they will expect you to give them a price.
  [1]
 If your price is too high, they will say =E2=80=9Cdone, you buy, pleasure =
doing business with you,=E2=80=9D and then you will own a bunch of bonds th=
at you overpaid for and that you will end up selling at a loss. If your pri=
ce is too low, they will say =E2=80=9Cugh, you are so bad at this, Morgan S=
tanley bid three points higher,=E2=80=9D you won=E2=80=99t do the trade and=
 you will lose a customer. You need to bid a price that is high enough that=
 the customer will want to trade, but low enough that you=E2=80=99ll make m=
oney. So you need to know what bonds are worth. This is partly a matter of =
long-term fundamental and economic analysis but=C2=A0it is also a matter of=
 short-term understanding of the market. If you know another customer is lo=
oking to buy the bonds at 87, you can bid 86.5 for them and make a quick 50=
 cents. Again, the phone number comes in handy: The more you talk to other =
customers, and know about their holdings and desires and trades, the better=
 you will be at setting a prices.=09
Money. If the customers call you up looking to sell you some bonds, and you=
 say =E2=80=9C86.5,=E2=80=9D and they say =E2=80=9Cdone, you buy one millio=
n at 86.5,=E2=80=9D then you will need to pay them $865,000 for the bonds. =
Then you will try to turn around and sell them to another customer for $870=
,000 and clip a quick $5,000, but you won=E2=80=99t=C2=A0always=C2=A0be abl=
e to do that. Lots of corporate bonds don=E2=80=99t trade all=C2=A0that=C2=
=A0often. You will need to be able to put many millions of dollars of your =
employer=E2=80=99s money into buying and holding bonds in inventory for a w=
hile, if you want to be in the bond dealing business.=C2=A0

Historically, in the US, this was a business done by investment banks and t=
rading firms;=C2=A0eventually=C2=A0it became largely a business done by big=
 universal Wall Street banks. Banks had famous brands and lots of salespeop=
le to take customers out to dinner and get to know their needs, so customer=
s knew to call them. They had lots of smart people who were good at pricing=
 bonds, and their customer flow gave them a good sense of market prices. An=
d, being banks, they had money.
Over the last decade or so, though, some of these advantages eroded. Bond t=
rading, like stock trading, became increasingly electronic, which means=C2=
=A0that you no longer need a sales force and a turret phone to trade bonds:=
 Investors increasingly go to electronic trading platforms to buy bonds, so=
 if you are on those platforms selling bonds, they will buy from you withou=
t much advertising or relationship-building. That in turn erodes=C2=A0the b=
anks=E2=80=99 information advantage: Anyone who trades on those platforms c=
an get some sense of who=E2=80=99s paying what for which bonds, and if you =
do enough trades then you can use computers to do statistical analysis and =
figure out what prices to quote, even without the gut feeling that bank dea=
lers get from long experience of talking to customers all day.
And the money =E2=80=A6=C2=A0well, banks used to have a lot of money to ris=
k on bond trading, because it was lucrative, and because it was what they d=
id. But after the 2008 financial crisis, regulatory capital requirements an=
d proprietary-trading restrictions limited their ability to put money into =
bond trading, and their own risk appetites were reduced. And so people were=
 worried about bond market liquidity: Banks used to be ready to buy any bon=
ds anyone wanted to sell, or sell any bonds anyone wanted to buy, but incre=
asingly they weren=E2=80=99t, so investors worried about their ability to b=
uy or sell bonds when they needed to. Increasingly, instead of buying and s=
elling bonds for their own inventory, banks would try to put together riskl=
ess trades, where they lined up both a buyer and a seller of the bonds and =
took a commission.
Meanwhile electronic proprietary trading firms had largely taken over stock=
 trading. Stock trading is perfect for electronic prop trading firms: Stock=
s trade electronically on exchanges, so you don=E2=80=99t need a sales forc=
e to be a dealer. The exchanges have lots of public trade and order book da=
ta, so you can build algorithms that are pretty good at figuring out the ri=
ght price to charge for a stock. And you don=E2=80=99t need all that much m=
oney to trade stocks, because stocks trade frequently and in small incremen=
ts.=C2=A0If you buy 100 shares of stock on the exchange, you can probably s=
ell them a second later. A prop trading firm could represent a large fracti=
on of the total volume on the stock exchange, and still go home every night=
 with no positions.
This has all been lucrative enough that, now, the prop trading firms have t=
ons of money. (Also they can borrow more from banks.) So they might as well=
 get into bonds. Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Alice Atkins and Katherine Doherty rep=
ort:

They subdued stocks, claimed a chunk of foreign exchange and muscled into t=
he commodity market. Now high-tech trading firms like Citadel Securities LL=
C and Jane Street are pushing deeper than ever into fixed income.
Riding a wave of digitization and a boom in ETFs, electronic market makers =
=E2=80=94 who keep securities moving by continuously buying and selling in =
lightning-fast transactions =E2=80=94 are expanding their reach in governme=
nt bond trading and finally gaining ground in the once-untouchable world of=
 corporate debt.
In the process they=E2=80=99re storming territory ruled by Wall Street=E2=
=80=99s biggest banks, throwing everything from client relationships to tra=
nsaction costs into flux and scooping up staff alongside market share =E2=
=80=94 even as the march of so-called electronification stirs concerns abou=
t financial stability.

Part of the shift is that you no longer need a phone
  [2]
:

With a market far more complicated than that of equities, no centralized ex=
changes and huge pockets of illiquidity, trading bonds of all stripes has l=
ong been a highly profitable affair largely conducted between banks and the=
ir clients via phone or message.
But under pressure from buy-side firms looking for cheaper, faster executio=
n, and as tighter regulation curtailed bank activity, trading platforms at =
the heart of the business began digitizing and opening up to new liquidity =
providers. Traders working from home during Covid lockdowns lent momentum t=
o this electronic shift.
Jane Street estimates in its debt document that the percentage of investmen=
t-grade bonds traded electronically in the US jumped to 42% in 2023 from 34=
% two years earlier. In high-yield debt it was 31%, up from 25%. Coalition =
Greenwich says 64% of US Treasury trades were handled electronically in 202=
3, up from 45% before the pandemic struck.

Part of it is that, with the rise of electronic trading, algorithms have mo=
re data and are better at pricing bonds:
=E2=80=9CQuantitative algorithms enable them to have a comprehensive and de=
tailed view of the market at any time,=E2=80=9D said Valerie Noel, head of =
trading at $26 billion Syz. That=E2=80=99s an =E2=80=9Cinvaluable strength =
for accurately pricing a wide range of worldwide bonds,=E2=80=9D she said.
Part of it is that these firms are now big enough to take on a lot of bond =
inventory
  [3]
:

The same tools also help them =E2=80=9Cinternalize=E2=80=9D more activity =
=E2=80=94 fill orders from their own inventory =E2=80=94 which can reduce a=
 transaction=E2=80=99s discernible impact on the market. That=E2=80=99s a b=
ig draw to asset managers who want to offload or establish positions withou=
t creating a stir. =E2=80=A6
At $67 billion Jupiter Asset Management, Mike Poole says the firm has been =
stepping up its use of non-bank liquidity over the last year after previous=
ly only ever trading with top tier investment banks. =E2=80=9CConsistency a=
nd having confidence that you can get something done at that level, while m=
inimizing leakage=E2=80=9D are the big draws, the head of trading said.

I will say that if you knew a lot about the corporate bond market in, like,=
 the 1960s, and then you knew nothing about the bond market since then, and=
 you tried to guess what the effect of 60 years of improved computer techno=
logy would have on bond markets, your guess might be pretty accurate? Like =
in some ways this is a story of =E2=80=9Cindependent electronic proprietary=
 trading firms are=C2=A0competing with Wall Street=E2=80=99s biggest banks =
for bond trades.=E2=80=9D But in other ways this is a story of =E2=80=9Cbon=
d dealers in the US used to be mostly independent proprietary trading firms=
, and of course in the 1960s those firms traded by telephone and priced bon=

 and price bonds using algorithms.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

      Private markets are the new public markets
   =20
 =20

We are at a weird transition point in private stock markets. In the olden d=
ays, big companies usually had to go public, because public markets were wh=
ere the money was. Public stock markets were far deeper than private ones, =
and so if you wanted to have a big company that could raise a lot of money,=
 you needed to sell stock to public investors.
That no longer seems to be all that true: There=E2=80=99s tons of money in =
private markets, from venture funds, private equity funds, SoftBank Group C=
orp., and public-market investors who are now also willing to invest in pri=
vate companies. Strictly from a=C2=A0fundraising=C2=A0perspective, big tech=
 companies can kind of stay private forever, and some do.
  [4]

On the other hand, when those companies originally issued stock =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0to early employees, to early venture capital investors =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0those investors were sort of operating under the old expectations. They =
figured their company would be private for a while, would work out its busi=
ness, and would either (1) fail and go to zero (a risk of the startup life!=
) or (2) succeed and go public (or sell for cash to a big acquirer). The em=
ployees and investors got private stock that they couldn=E2=80=99t sell, bu=
t there was an implicit expectation that they=E2=80=99d=C2=A0eventually=C2=
=A0be able to sell it, within, you know, five to 10 years.
Also even if you=E2=80=99ve been private for a decade and are raising money=
 at a $50 billion valuation, the new investors will also expect=C2=A0some=
=C2=A0ability to exit,=C2=A0someday.
I suppose you could have three models for how this eventually goes:
=09This is all a small blip, big tech companies will all still go public, b=
ut the timeline will be a bit longer, and employees and venture capitalists=
 will just have to be a bit more patient. (The employees might want to dema=
nd a bit more cash compensation and a bit less stock.)=09Many companies wil=
l really stay private forever, or for a long time, but private secondary ma=
rkets will develop where early employees and investors can sell their share=
s. Those secondary markets will be a bit different from the public markets =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0they=E2=80=99ll be limited to accredited investors, maybe th=
ey=E2=80=99ll ban short selling, the fees will be higher, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but basically there=E2=80=99ll be an expectation that the early employee=
s and investors exit through private markets instead of public ones.=C2=A0=
=09Many companies will stay private forever, and they=C2=A0won=E2=80=99t=C2=
=A0allow secondary markets: They=E2=80=99ll want to keep complete control o=
ver who owns their stock and how they buy and sell it. Of course early empl=
oyees and investors will still need liquidity, but they=E2=80=99ll get it o=
nly through the company: The company will raise money from new investors, a=
nd will use the money to do tender offers to buy shares from old investors =
and employees.
  [5]
=C2=A0The company will control the market in its own stock, deciding how mu=
ch to buy and how much to sell, and standing in between each trade.

Those models are not really exclusive; right now they all more or less coex=
ist. But some companies push harder for the third model, running big tender=
 offers but restricting secondary markets. At the New York Times, Erin Grif=
fith writes about how =E2=80=9Ctensions over the shadowy and often enigmati=
c market of private company stocks have reached a boiling point=E2=80=9D:

The market for private company stocks, also known as the secondary market, =
is on track to hit a record $64 billion this year, up 40 percent from last =
year, according to Sacra, a research firm focused on private investments. A=
 decade ago, the private company stock market was roughly $16 billion, acco=
rding to Industry Ventures, a firm focused on secondary transactions. ...
Now, as these start-ups mature and don=E2=80=99t appear to be in a rush to =
go public, a wider range of investors are becoming eager to own their stock=
. New online marketplaces that match sellers of start-up stock with interes=
ted buyers have sprung up. =E2=80=A6
The activity has increasingly rattled some start-ups, which have long resis=
ted letting their shares freely change hands. =E2=80=A6
Stripe, valued at $65 billion in the private market, has issued a strongly =
worded statement about offers to buy its stock. Any offer to invest in its =
shares that does not come from the company is =E2=80=9Cvery likely a scam,=
=E2=80=9D it said. Stripe has encouraged shareholders to report such offers=
 to law enforcement.
Some companies that have stayed private the longest, including Stripe, whic=
h is 14 years old, and SpaceX, which is 22 years old, have begun offering r=
egular opportunities for employees to sell a portion of their stock at a se=
t price.

It does seem like a headache for a company to spend a lot of time and energ=
y coordinating trades of its stock; why not let an outside marketplace do t=
hat? On the other hand, if you are running a startup with an 11-digit valua=
tion, you are probably quite particular about your product and user experie=
nce, and you might not want to give up control of something as fundamental =
as who owns your stock.=C2=A0

Classic =E2=80=9Copen=C2=A0market=E2=80=9D stock=C2=A0manipulation
  [6]
 works more or less like this:
=09You buy a ton of a stock (commodity, derivative, crypto token, whatever)=
, in a sloppy and aggressive way, with the intention of pushing its price u=
p as much as possible. (Or you sell it to push the price down.) This costs =
you money: You are overpaying for the stock, to move its price higher.=09Wh=
en the price is high, you extract some money from some related market. Perh=
aps you borrow money against your stock. Or you had some derivative tied to=
 the stock price, and you make money on that. You push the price up at quar=
ter-end, allowing you to extract a big performance fee from your clients or=
 a big bonus from your bank. Your selling the stock to push it down somehow=
 causes the company to go bankrupt. Lots of ways to accomplish this.=09The =
point is that the money you extract=C2=A0in Step 2 is supposed to be more t=
han the money you spent in Step 1.

You=E2=80=99re doing the manipulation to make money, and just pushing up th=
e price of the stock, on its own, doesn=E2=80=99t make you any money. Howev=
er, there is another, degenerate form of market manipulation that works lik=
e this:
=09You buy a ton of a stock, intending to push the price up. This costs you=
 money.=09When the price is high, you are like =E2=80=9Chahahaha, this pric=
e is high. Wheeee!=E2=80=9D=09The amusement you got in Step 2 is, ideally, =
worth the price you paid for it in Step 1.

I have written a few times around here about my friend Sarah Meyohas, who d=
id an art project where she manipulated penny stocks, painted the resulting=
 stock charts, and sold the paintings to collectors. The manipulation was n=
ot for profit, but for art. Though I assume she made more selling the paint=
ings than she spent on the penny stocks, so it=E2=80=99s a little ambiguous=
. In any case she never got in trouble for it. (=E2=80=9CHer show opens ton=
ight,=E2=80=9D I wrote in 2016,=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cand you should go see it, esp=
ecially if you work for the Securities and Exchange Commission.=E2=80=9D)
We also talked once about a guy named James Gubb, who traded a stock back a=
nd forth with himself =E2=80=9Cin order to create the image of an =E2=80=98=
up yours=E2=80=99 middle finger in the price chart.=E2=80=9D He was fined b=
y South African regulators for creating an artificial price. But his manipu=
lation was for art, or political protest, not for profit.
And then of course there is GameStop! I don=E2=80=99t know.
Bill Hwang goes on trial tomorrow for alleged criminal market manipulation =
in connection with the collapse of his family office, Archegos Capital Mana=
gement. Hwang did some trades that look like=C2=A0possible market manipulat=
ion: He plowed tons of borrowed money into a dozen stocks, buying rapidly a=
nd sloppily and pushing the prices up dramatically. This had the result of =
briefly inflating the value of Archegos=E2=80=99s portfolio to something li=
ke $36 billion.
Had he then called up his banks and said =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like to borro=
w $9 billion against this $36 billion pile of stock=E2=80=9D and buried the=
 money in his backyard, fine, great, obvious market manipulation. But as fa=
r as I can tell, he did not. Eventually the stocks went down, Archegos coll=
apsed and he lost approximately all of his money. The whole thing is just a=
 mystery. Did he manipulate the stocks up and extract money that we haven=
=E2=80=99t learned about yet? Did he manipulate the stocks up intending to =
extract money, and then mess up the endgame? Did he=C2=A0not=C2=A0manipulat=
e the stocks: Was he just buying them, aggressively to be sure, but honestl=
y, because he liked them and thought they were undervalued? Or did he manip=
ulate the stocks for no reason, or for weird personal reasons?
Everyone wants to know, including the judge:

=E2=80=9CWhat I=E2=80=99m trying to figure out in my mind: to what end,=E2=
=80=9D Alvin Hellerstein, the veteran judge overseeing the case asked last =
year, after prosecutors outlined how Hwang had covertly and artificially dr=
iven up the stock price of a handful of listed companies, only for them to =
later plummet. =E2=80=9CHe lost his money.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6
=E2=80=9CWhat was in it for him?=E2=80=9D the 90-year-old judge asked. =E2=
=80=9CBeing a big shot, I suppose that=E2=80=99s possible, but it doesn=E2=
=80=99t seem to me that was his aim.=E2=80=9D

And:

From the start, prosecutors have been vague in their explanation of the cri=
minal contours of Hwang=E2=80=99s plan. Asked by Hellerstein during one pre=
-trial hearing whether the trader had engaged in a =E2=80=9Cpump and dump=

iverse type thinking=E2=80=9D that caused him to harbour =E2=80=9Cvisions o=

I don=E2=80=99t know! Profitable off-ramps, sure, that sounds like manipula=

not justified by the fundamentals, because I wanted to brag about it,=E2=80=
=9D then I suppose that would qualify as market manipulation, but it is a t=

 that a company with more than 500 shareholders *had* to go public under US=
 law. But in 2012, that number was increased to 2,000; more importantly, th=
e number applies to =E2=80=9Cholders of record,=E2=80=9D and it=E2=80=99s r=
elatively easy to avoid having 2,000 holders of record. So it=E2=80=99s rar=
e, now, to be forced to go public by these rules.


  [5] Or it will coordinate tenders where the new investors buy directly fr=
om the old ones.


  [6] This appears to be the term in US law for market manipulation that do=
es *not* involve going around and lying about a stock. More common forms of=
 market manipulation are, like, you buy some stock and=20
put out a fake press release saying the company is being taken over, etc.


  [7] Though if you have not seen Demi Adejuyigbe=E2=80=99s contribution, I=
 can at least help you with that.

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Bond trading</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Traditionally there are three main things you =
need=C2=A0to be a good <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbG=
VzLzIwMjQtMDItMDcvaGVkZ2UtZnVuZHMtYXJlLWRlYWxlcnMtbm93P2NtcGlkPUJCRDA1MDcyN=
F9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90=
ZXJtPTI0MDUwNyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf8a=
82699" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;">bond dealer</a>:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">
<em>A listed phone number</em>. As a dealer, your job is to buy bonds from =
people who want to sell them, and sell bonds to people who want to buy them=
. You rely on customers for your flow, and bonds have historically been a v=
oice-traded market. You need customers to know that you are in the bond-dea=
ling business, and to think of you when they want to trade bonds.=C2=A0</li=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">
<em>Some idea of what bonds are worth</em>. If the customers do call you up=
 looking to sell you some bonds, they will expect you to give them a price.=
<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; t=
ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: trans=
parent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a> If your price is too high, they will say =E2=80=9Cdone, you buy, pleas=
ure doing business with you,=E2=80=9D and then you will own a bunch of bond=
s that you overpaid for and that you will end up selling at a loss. If your=
 price is too low, they will say =E2=80=9Cugh, you are so bad at this, Morg=
an Stanley bid three points higher,=E2=80=9D you won=E2=80=99t do the trade=
 and you will lose a customer. You need to bid a price that is high enough =
that the customer will want to trade, but low enough that you=E2=80=99ll ma=
ke money. So you need to know what bonds are worth. This is partly a matter=
 of long-term fundamental and economic analysis but=C2=A0it is also a matte=
r of short-term understanding of the market. If you know another customer i=
s looking to buy the bonds at 87, you can bid 86.5 for them and make a quic=
k 50 cents. Again, the phone number comes in handy: The more you talk to ot=
her customers, and know about their holdings and desires and trades, the be=
tter you will be at setting a prices.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5p=
x;">
<em>Money</em>. If the customers call you up looking to sell you some bonds=
, and you say =E2=80=9C86.5,=E2=80=9D and they say =E2=80=9Cdone, you buy o=
ne million at 86.5,=E2=80=9D then you will need to pay them $865,000 for th=
e bonds. Then you will try to turn around and sell them to another customer=
 for $870,000 and clip a quick $5,000, but you won=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>alway=
s=C2=A0</em>be able to do that. Lots of corporate bonds don=E2=80=99t trade=
 all=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>often. You will need to be able to put many mi=
llions of dollars of your employer=E2=80=99s money into buying and holding =
bonds in inventory for a while, if you want to be in the bond dealing busin=
ess.=C2=A0</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Historically, in the US, this was a business d=
one by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35296383.28=
5345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubmV3eW9ya2ZlZC5vcmcvbWVkaWFsaWJyYXJ5L21lZGlhL3Jlc2Vhcm=
NoL3N0YWZmX3JlcG9ydHMvc3I3NzcucGRmP2xhPWVu/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB59f77f5=
b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">investment banks and trading firms</a>;=C2=A0<a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly9zY2hvbGF=
yc2hpcC5sYXcuY29sdW1iaWEuZWR1L2NnaS92aWV3Y29udGVudC5jZ2k_YXJ0aWNsZT01MTExJm=
NvbnRleHQ9ZmFjdWx0eV9zY2hvbGFyc2hpcA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBac1f8108" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">eventually</a>=C2=A0it became largely a business done by big universal=
 Wall Street banks. Banks had famous brands and lots of salespeople to take=
 customers out to dinner and get to know their needs, so customers knew to =
call them. They had lots of smart people who were good at pricing bonds, an=
d their customer flow gave them a good sense of market prices. And, being b=
anks, they had money.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Over the last decade or so, though, some of th=
ese advantages eroded. Bond trading, like stock trading, became increasingl=
y electronic, which means=C2=A0that <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlua=
W9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDItMDcvaGVkZ2UtZnVuZHMtYXJlLWRlYWxlcnMtbm93P2NtcGlk=
PUJCRDA1MDcyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV=
0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDUwNyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b852=
4a319a23cCf8a82699" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">you no longer need a sales force and a turret=
 phone</a> to trade bonds: Investors increasingly go to electronic trading =
platforms to buy bonds, so if you are on those platforms selling bonds, the=
y will buy from you without much advertising or relationship-building. That=
 in turn erodes=C2=A0the banks=E2=80=99 information advantage: Anyone who t=
rades on those platforms can get some sense of who=E2=80=99s paying what fo=
r which bonds, and if you do enough trades then you can use computers to do=
 statistical analysis and figure out what prices to quote, even without the=
 gut feeling that bank dealers get from long experience of talking to custo=
mers all day.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And the money =E2=80=A6=C2=A0well, banks used =
to have a lot of money to risk on bond trading, because it was lucrative, a=
nd because it was what they did. But after the 2008 financial crisis, regul=
atory capital requirements and proprietary-trading restrictions limited the=
ir ability to put money into bond trading, and their own risk appetites wer=
e reduced. And so <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzI=
wMTUtMDYtMDMvcGVvcGxlLWFyZS13b3JyaWVkLWFib3V0LWJvbmQtbWFya2V0LWxpcXVpZGl0eT=
9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNTA3MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9b=
mV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA1MDcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cBd5f04441" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" t=
arget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: solid; background-color: #ccc;">people were worried about bond market li=
quidity</a>: Banks used to be ready to buy any bonds anyone wanted to sell,=
 or sell any bonds anyone wanted to buy, but increasingly they weren=E2=80=
=99t, so investors worried about their ability to buy or sell bonds when th=
ey needed to. Increasingly, instead of buying and selling bonds for their o=
wn inventory, banks would try to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS92aWV3L2Fy=
dGljbGVzLzIwMTctMTItMDMvYm9uZC1pbnZlc3RvcnMtYXJlLXdvcnJpZWQtYWJvdXQtYm9uZC1=
tYXJrZXQtbGlxdWlkaXR5P2NtcGlkPUJCRDA1MDcyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW=
1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDUwNyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249b=
W9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9c7fb04c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" ite=
mscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">put together riskl=
ess trades</a>, where they lined up both a buyer and a seller of the bonds =
and took a commission.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile electronic proprietary trading firms=
 had largely taken over stock trading. Stock trading is perfect for electro=
nic prop trading firms: Stocks trade electronically on exchanges, so you do=
n=E2=80=99t need a sales force to be a dealer. The exchanges have lots of p=
ublic trade and order book data, so you can build algorithms that are prett=
y good at figuring out the right price to charge for a stock. And you don=
=E2=80=99t need all <em>that </em>much money to trade stocks, because stock=
s trade frequently and in small increments.=C2=A0If you buy 100 shares of s=
tock on the exchange, you can probably sell them a second later. A prop tra=
ding firm could represent a large fraction of the total volume on the stock=
 exchange, and still go home every night with no positions.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This has all been lucrative enough that, now, =
the prop trading firms have tons of money. (Also they can <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmx=
vb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDMtMDQvdHJhZGluZy1kZXNrcy10cm=
FkZS1sZXNzLWxlbmQtbW9yZT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNTA3MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtP=
WVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA1MDcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWdu=
PW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBa0d64830" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" i=
temscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">borrow more from=
 banks</a>.) So they might as well get into bonds. Bloomberg=E2=80=99s <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDUtMDYvamFuZS1zdHJlZ=
XQtYW5kLWNpdGFkZWwtc2VjdXJpdGllcy1yYWNlLXRvLWdyYWItYm9uZC1tYXJrZXQtdHVyZj9j=
bXBpZD1CQkQwNTA3MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV=
3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA1MDcmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB94864f00" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">Alice Atkins and Katherine Doherty report<=
/a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">They subdued stocks, claimed a chunk of foreig=
n exchange and muscled into the commodity market. Now high-tech trading fir=
ms like Citadel Securities LLC and Jane Street are pushing deeper than ever=
 into fixed income.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Riding a wave of digitization and a boom in ET=
Fs, electronic market makers =E2=80=94 who keep securities moving by contin=
uously buying and selling in lightning-fast transactions =E2=80=94 are expa=
nding their reach in government bond trading and finally gaining ground in =
the once-untouchable world of corporate debt.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the process they=E2=80=99re storming territ=
ory ruled by Wall Street=E2=80=99s biggest banks, throwing everything from =
client relationships to transaction costs into flux and scooping up staff a=
longside market share =E2=80=94 even as the march of so-called electronific=
ation stirs concerns about financial stability.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Part of the shift is that you no longer need a=
 phone<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000=
000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bot=
tom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color:=
 transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With a market far more complicated than that o=
f equities, no centralized exchanges and huge pockets of illiquidity, tradi=
ng bonds of all stripes has long been a highly profitable affair largely co=
nducted between banks and their clients via phone or message.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But under pressure from buy-side firms looking=
 for cheaper, faster execution, and as tighter regulation curtailed bank ac=
tivity, trading platforms at the heart of the business began digitizing and=
 opening up to new liquidity providers. Traders working from home during Co=
vid lockdowns lent momentum to this electronic shift.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Jane Street estimates in its debt document tha=
t the percentage of investment-grade bonds traded electronically in the US =
jumped to 42% in 2023 from 34% two years earlier. In high-yield debt it was=
 31%, up from 25%. Coalition Greenwich says 64% of US Treasury trades were =
handled electronically in 2023, up from 45% before the pandemic struck.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Part of it is that, with the rise of electroni=
c trading, algorithms have more data and are better at pricing bonds:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CQuantitative algorithms e=
nable them to have a comprehensive and detailed view of the market at any t=
ime,=E2=80=9D said Valerie Noel, head of trading at $26 billion Syz. That=
=E2=80=99s an =E2=80=9Cinvaluable strength for accurately pricing a wide ra=
nge of worldwide bonds,=E2=80=9D she said.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Part of it is that these firms are now big eno=
ugh to take on a lot of bond inventory<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#=
footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: no=
ne !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-bl=
ock;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The same tools also help them =E2=80=9Cinterna=
lize=E2=80=9D more activity =E2=80=94 fill orders from their own inventory =
=E2=80=94 which can reduce a transaction=E2=80=99s discernible impact on th=
e market. That=E2=80=99s a big draw to asset managers who want to offload o=
r establish positions without creating a stir. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At $67 billion Jupiter Asset Management, Mike =
Poole says the firm has been stepping up its use of non-bank liquidity over=
 the last year after previously only ever trading with top tier investment =
banks. =E2=80=9CConsistency and having confidence that you can get somethin=
g done at that level, while minimizing leakage=E2=80=9D are the big draws, =
the head of trading said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I will say that if you knew a lot about the co=
rporate bond market in, like, the 1960s, and then you knew nothing about th=
e bond market since then, and you tried to guess what the effect of 60 year=
s of improved computer technology would have on bond markets, your guess mi=
ght be pretty accurate? Like in some ways this is a story of =E2=80=9Cindep=
endent electronic proprietary trading firms are=C2=A0competing with Wall St=
reet=E2=80=99s biggest banks for bond trades.=E2=80=9D But in other ways th=
is is a story of =E2=80=9Cbond dealers in the US used to be mostly independ=
ent proprietary trading firms, and of course in the 1960s those firms trade=
d by telephone and priced bonds using gut instinct and connoisseurship, whi=
le now they trade by computer and price bonds using algorithms.=E2=80=9D=C2=
=A0</p>

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Private markets are the new public markets</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We are at a weird transition point in private =
stock markets. In the olden days, big companies usually had to go public, b=
ecause public markets were where the money was. Public stock markets were f=
ar deeper than private ones, and so if you wanted to have a big company tha=
t could raise a lot of money, you needed to sell stock to public investors.=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That no longer seems to be all that true: Ther=
e=E2=80=99s tons of money in private markets, from venture funds, private e=
quity funds, SoftBank Group Corp., and public-market investors who are now =
also willing to invest in private companies. Strictly from a=C2=A0<em>fundr=
aising=C2=A0</em>perspective, big tech companies can kind of stay private f=
orever, and some do.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; ba=
ckground-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the other hand, when those companies origin=
ally issued stock =E2=80=94=C2=A0to early employees, to early venture capit=
al investors =E2=80=94=C2=A0those investors were sort of operating under th=
e old expectations. They figured their company would be private for a while=
, would work out its business, and would either (1) fail and go to zero (a =
risk of the startup life!) or (2) succeed and go public (or sell for cash t=
o a big acquirer). The employees and investors got private stock that they =
couldn=E2=80=99t sell, but there was an implicit expectation that they=E2=
=80=99d=C2=A0<em>eventually=C2=A0</em>be able to sell it, within, you know,=
 five to 10 years.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also even if you=E2=80=99ve been private for a=
 decade and are raising money at a $50 billion valuation, the new investors=
 will also expect=C2=A0<em>some=C2=A0</em>ability to exit,=C2=A0<em>someday=
</em>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose you could have three models for how =
this eventually goes:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">This is all a small blip, big tech co=
mpanies will all still go public, but the timeline will be a bit longer, an=
d employees and venture capitalists will just have to be a bit more patient=
. (The employees might want to demand a bit more cash compensation and a bi=
t less stock.)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Many companies will=
 really stay private forever, or for a long time, but private secondary mar=
kets will develop where early employees and investors can sell their shares=
. Those secondary markets will be a bit different from the public markets =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0they=E2=80=99ll be limited to accredited investors, maybe th=
ey=E2=80=99ll ban short selling, the fees will be higher, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but basically there=E2=80=99ll be an expectation that the early employee=
s and investors exit through private markets instead of public ones.=C2=A0<=
/li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Many companies will stay private f=
orever, and they=C2=A0<em>won=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>allow secondary markets: =
They=E2=80=99ll want to keep complete control over who owns their stock and=
 how they buy and sell it. Of course early employees and investors will sti=
ll need liquidity, but they=E2=80=99ll get it only through the company: The=
 company will raise money from new investors, and will use the money to do =
tender offers to buy shares from old investors and employees.<a class=3D"fo=
otnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !import=
ant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0The company will control the market in its own stock, deciding ho=
w much to buy and how much to sell, and standing in between each trade.</li=
>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Those models are not really exclusive; right n=
ow they all more or less coexist. But some companies push harder for the th=
ird model, running big tender offers but restricting secondary markets. At =
the New York Times, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=
k/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMjAyNC8wNS8wNi90ZWNobm9sb=
2d5L3N0YXJ0dXAtc3RvY2stZnVuZHMtZGVzdGlueS5odG1s/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB05=
a78fd8" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" st=
yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;">Erin Griffith writes</a> about how =E2=80=9Ctensions over t=
he shadowy and often enigmatic market of private company stocks have reache=
d a boiling point=E2=80=9D:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The market for private company stocks, also kn=
own as the secondary market, is on track to hit a record $64 billion this y=
ear, up 40 percent from last year, according to Sacra, a research firm focu=
sed on private investments. A decade ago, the private company stock market =
was roughly $16 billion, according to Industry Ventures, a firm focused on =
secondary transactions. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, as these start-ups mature and don=E2=80=
=99t appear to be in a rush to go public, a wider range of investors are be=
coming eager to own their stock. New online marketplaces that match sellers=
 of start-up stock with interested buyers have sprung up. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The activity has increasingly rattled some sta=
rt-ups, which have long resisted letting their shares freely change hands. =
=E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Stripe, valued at $65 billion in the private m=
arket, has issued a strongly worded statement about offers to buy its stock=
. Any offer to invest in its shares that does not come from the company is =
=E2=80=9Cvery likely a scam,=E2=80=9D it said. Stripe has encouraged shareh=
olders to report such offers to law enforcement.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some companies that have stayed private the lo=
ngest, including Stripe, which is 14 years old, and SpaceX, which is 22 yea=
rs old, have begun offering regular opportunities for employees to sell a p=
ortion of their stock at a set price.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It does seem like a headache for a company to =
spend a lot of time and energy coordinating trades of its stock; why not le=
t an outside marketplace do that? On the other hand, if you are running a s=
tartup with an 11-digit valuation, you are probably quite particular about =
your product and user experience, and you might not want to give up control=
 of something as fundamental as who owns your stock.=C2=A0</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Classic =E2=80=9Copen=C2=A0market=E2=80=9D sto=
ck=C2=A0manipulation<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; ba=
ckground-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a> works more or less like this:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You buy a ton of a stock (commodity, =
derivative, crypto token, whatever), in a sloppy and aggressive way, with t=
he intention of pushing its price up as much as possible. (Or you sell it t=
o push the price down.) This costs you money: You are overpaying for the st=
ock, to move its price higher.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Whe=
n the price is high, you <em>extract </em>some money from some related mark=
et. Perhaps you borrow money against your stock. Or you had some derivative=
 tied to the stock price, and you make money on that. You push the price up=
 at quarter-end, allowing you to extract a big performance fee from your cl=
ients or a big bonus from your bank. Your selling the stock to push it down=
 somehow causes the company to go bankrupt. Lots of ways to accomplish this=
.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The point is that the money you =
extract=C2=A0in Step 2 is supposed to be more than the money you spent in S=
tep 1.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You=E2=80=99re doing the manipulation to make =
money, and just pushing up the price of the stock, on its own, doesn=E2=80=
=99t make you any money. However, there is another, degenerate form of mark=
et manipulation that works like this:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You buy a ton of a stock, intending t=
o push the price up. This costs you money.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-botto=
m: 5px;">When the price is high, you are like =E2=80=9Chahahaha, this price=
 is high. Wheeee!=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The amu=
sement you got in Step 2 is, ideally, worth the price you paid for it in St=
ep 1.</li>
</ol>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We also <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bill Hwang <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=

ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">goes on trial tom=
orrow</a> for alleged criminal market manipulation in connection with the c=
ollapse of his family office, Archegos Capital Management. Hwang did some t=
rades that <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3529638=
3.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMD=
QtMjcvYmlsbC1od2FuZy1hcnJlc3QtaW5zaWRlLXRoZS12aWNpb3VzLWN5Y2xlLW9mLWFyY2hlZ=
29zLWNvbGxhcHNlP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA1MDcyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwm=
dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDUwNyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXl=
zdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5a8c9d20" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">look like=C2=A0<em>possi=
ble</em> market manipulation</a>: He plowed tons of borrowed money into a d=
ozen stocks, buying rapidly and sloppily and pushing the prices up dramatic=
ally. This had the result of briefly inflating the value of Archegos=E2=80=
=99s portfolio to something like $36 billion.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Had he then called up his banks and said =E2=
=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like to borrow $9 billion against this $36 billion pile o=
f stock=E2=80=9D and buried the money in his backyard, fine, great, obvious=
 market manipulation. But as far as I can tell, he did not. Eventually the =
stocks went down, Archegos collapsed and he lost approximately all of his m=
oney. The whole thing is just a mystery. Did he manipulate the stocks up an=
d extract money that we haven=E2=80=99t learned about yet? Did he manipulat=
e the stocks up intending to extract money, and then mess up the endgame? D=
id he=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>manipulate the stocks: Was he just buying them=
, aggressively to be sure, but honestly, because he liked them and thought =
they were undervalued? Or did he manipulate the stocks for no reason, or fo=
r weird personal reasons?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Everyone wants to know, <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29t=
L2NvbnRlbnQvY2ZkYzAxN2UtYWI0Mi00ZDZjLTg2MjYtMjY0OGVjZDIzN2Jm/607f07ceef4b85=
24a319a23cBd5a9b0bf" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">including the judge</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWhat I=E2=80=99m trying to figure out=
 in my mind: to what end,=E2=80=9D Alvin Hellerstein, the veteran judge ove=
rseeing the case asked last year, after prosecutors outlined how Hwang had =
covertly and artificially driven up the stock price of a handful of listed =
companies, only for them to later plummet. =E2=80=9CHe lost his money.=E2=
=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWhat was in it for him?=E2=80=9D the =
90-year-old judge asked. =E2=80=9CBeing a big shot, I suppose that=E2=80=99=
s possible, but it doesn=E2=80=99t seem to me that was his aim.=E2=80=9D</p=
>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">From the start, prosecutors have been vague in=
 their explanation of the criminal contours of Hwang=E2=80=99s plan. Asked =
by Hellerstein during one pre-trial hearing whether the trader had engaged =
in a =E2=80=9Cpump and dump=E2=80=9D scheme, assistant US attorney Andrew T=
homas offered that Hwang had rather been involved in a =E2=80=9Cpump and br=
ag=E2=80=9D enterprise, and said witnesses at trial would =E2=80=9Cdescribe=
 the sort of king of the universe type thinking=E2=80=9D that caused him to=
 harbour =E2=80=9Cvisions of grandeur=E2=80=9D.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An alternative theory was offered by former Ar=
chegos managing director Brendan Sullivan, who alleged in a separate civil =
lawsuit brought in 2022 that the investment group transferred shares worth =
hundreds of millions of dollars to Hwang=E2=80=99s Grace and Mercy Foundati=
on that was to be used as a financial =E2=80=9Cescape pod=E2=80=9D. Sulliva=
n is expected to testify at the trial.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The government has hinted that it will also of=
fer evidence at trial of how Hwang sought =E2=80=9Cprofitable off-ramps=E2=
=80=9D for his Archegos trades, and that it will =E2=80=9Cfill in some of t=
he context=E2=80=9D while making its case to a jury.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know! Profitable off-ramps, su=
re, that sounds like manipulation. (Even if he failed to find them.) The ch=
arity as an =E2=80=9Cescape pod=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0a possibility we <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35296383.285345/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDUtMDEvYmFua3M=
tYXJlLXN0aWxsLXdoZXJlLXRoZS1tb25leS1pc24tdD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNTA3MjRfTU9ORVlTVF=
VGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA1M=

rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc=
;">discussed last week</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0could also sound manipulate-y.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CPump and brag=E2=80=9D? I mean? If yo=
u had Bill Hwang admitting in writing =E2=80=9CI manipulated these stocks u=
p to prices that I knew were not justified by the fundamentals, because I w=
anted to brag about it,=E2=80=9D then I suppose that would qualify as marke=
t manipulation, but it is a truly wild thing to assert about a=C2=A0<em>$36=
 billion=C2=A0</em>portfolio. That is a lot of money to spend on =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0nothing? It is true that there=E2=80=99s something=C2=A0<em>cool=C2=
=A0</em>about Hwang having built a $36 billion fortune and lost it all in a=
 week; like, that is a great story to tell at parties. (Or in prison?) But =
who sets out to do that?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But =E2=80=9CKing of the universe type thinkin=
g=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cvisions of grandeur=E2=80=9D? Don=E2=80=99t those s=
ound like =E2=80=9Cnot manipulation=E2=80=9D? Hwang=E2=80=99s defense will =
be of the form =E2=80=9CI liked the stocks and thought they were undervalue=
d,=E2=80=9D and if you believe that then I think his aggressive buying does=
 not count as manipulation. Manipulation here is a matter of intent; you=E2=
=80=99re allowed to buy stocks that you think are undervalued, even if your=
 buying pushes the stocks up. (Not legal advice!) Obviously Hwang was in an=
 important sense=C2=A0<em>wrong=C2=A0</em>about the stocks being undervalue=
d =E2=80=94=C2=A0once he stopped buying, the prices collapsed =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but the question is whether he believed in what he was doing. If he had =
=E2=80=9Cvisions of grandeur=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cking of the universe typ=
e thinking,=E2=80=9D doesn=E2=80=99t that just mean that he=C2=A0<em>did=C2=
=A0</em>believe he was right?=C2=A0</p>
